eyebrow: A useful reply should create movement
headline: Send the snag; I'll return a route with checkpoints.
subheadline: Write where progress stopped in your own words. I'll map a starting move, a checkpoint that tells us what changed, and the branch to take from there.
chatStarter: Rustam, this is the part I keep circling. Here is what I tried and where movement stopped.
whyItWorks: A route with checkpoints feels actionable while leaving room to adapt as evidence arrives.

eyebrow: Let reality narrow the field
headline: Name the constraint; let unsuitable options fall away.
subheadline: Paste the options and the limit none of them can violate. I'll use that constraint to remove poor fits and explain what remains.
chatStarter: Rustam, these choices all look reasonable until I apply this constraint.
whyItWorks: Filtering by a real constraint reduces choice without pretending there is a universal best answer.

eyebrow: Make success small enough to inspect
headline: Draw a small success box around the rough goal.
subheadline: Send the goal as it currently sounds. Together we'll mark what must be true, what is outside the box, and what evidence would count as useful progress.
chatStarter: Rustam, I know the outcome I want, but the goal keeps expanding every time I explain it.
proofLine: Start with a free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: The success-box metaphor turns a broad ambition into visible boundaries without shrinking its purpose.

eyebrow: A range needs a reason to change
headline: Give the estimate a range and revision trigger.
subheadline: Paste the estimate and what it assumes. I'll separate the steadier work from the volatile part, then name the evidence that should make the range move.
chatStarter: Rustam, I have a number, but I do not trust the assumptions holding it together.
whyItWorks: A range plus a revision trigger gives the client an estimate they can update instead of defend blindly.

eyebrow: Find where the run first changed
headline: Trace the failed run to its first divergence.
subheadline: Share the last known good run and the failing run. We'll compare their sequence and identify the earliest observable difference worth testing.
chatStarter: Rustam, the same flow used to work. Here is the last good run and the first bad run I can show.
whyItWorks: The earliest divergence narrows investigation without claiming it is already the root cause.

eyebrow: Keep the promise, reduce the surface
headline: Tight deadlines deserve a smaller promise, clearly kept.
subheadline: Tell me what the deadline cannot move and what the client must still receive. We'll cut scope around that outcome and state the smaller promise plainly.
chatStarter: Rustam, the date is fixed, but the current scope no longer fits it.
whyItWorks: It replaces deadline panic with a specific scope conversation centered on the promise that still matters.

eyebrow: The artifact should carry its context
headline: Make the handoff usable without a rescue meeting.
subheadline: Paste the handoff as the receiver sees it. I'll add the missing context, acceptance signal, and owner only where the work would otherwise stall.
chatStarter: Rustam, I handed this over, but the same questions keep coming back to me.
whyItWorks: The promise is practical: fewer context gaps, with the handoff itself as the first artifact to improve.

eyebrow: Prioritize by downstream drag
headline: Find the backlog item that keeps creating work.
subheadline: Paste the crowded list and point out what keeps resurfacing in support, rework, or manual cleanup. I'll help identify the item whose delay is generating more work.
chatStarter: Rustam, our backlog is long, but a few unresolved things keep coming back through other tasks.
proofLine: Start with a free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: It selects by recurring downstream cost, giving prioritization a concrete reason beyond urgency or volume.

eyebrow: Temporary fixes leave a recurring trail
headline: The workaround reveals what maintenance really costs.
subheadline: Describe the workaround and every recurring touch it requires. Together we'll map the time, failure points, and attention it consumes, then decide whether to contain or replace it.
chatStarter: Rustam, this workaround works, but we keep paying for it in small interruptions.
whyItWorks: Naming recurring maintenance turns a vague annoyance into a visible keep-or-replace tradeoff.

eyebrow: A useful choice includes the way out
headline: Map how your data leaves the chosen tool.
subheadline: Tell me which tool you're considering and where the important data lives. I'll trace export, ownership, dependencies, and the point where switching would become painful.
chatStarter: Rustam, this tool is convenient now, but I need to understand what leaving it would involve.
whyItWorks: The exit map makes lock-in concrete while the client still has room to choose.

eyebrow: Ready should be visible
headline: Make launch readiness observable, not a shared feeling.
subheadline: Share what is making launch feel unsafe. We'll turn that concern into a short set of observable checks and name what can wait.
chatStarter: Rustam, everyone has a different feeling about whether this is ready to launch.
whyItWorks: Observable checks replace group mood with evidence while keeping the launch conversation bounded.

eyebrow: Translate frustration into a broken moment
headline: The complaint reveals where your product's promise breaks.
subheadline: Paste the customer's words without cleaning them up. I'll connect the complaint to the expectation they carried and the exact moment the experience stopped matching it.
chatStarter: Rustam, this complaint sounds emotional, but I think it points to a specific promise we failed to keep.
whyItWorks: It respects the customer's language and produces an inspectable product moment instead of a defensive reply.

eyebrow: Start with use, not novelty
headline: Anchor the AI idea to a recurring moment.
subheadline: Tell me when the user reaches for help, what context exists, and what useful change should follow. We'll anchor the AI to that moment—or learn it has no job.
chatStarter: Rustam, I want to use AI here, but I cannot yet name the moment when it would help.
proofLine: Start with a free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: A recurring user moment gives the idea a trigger, context, and outcome without promising that AI belongs everywhere.

eyebrow: Translate implementation risk into ownership
headline: Turn a developer caveat into a client choice.
subheadline: Paste the caveat exactly as written. I'll separate what engineering can verify from what only you can choose, then phrase that choice in plain language.
chatStarter: Rustam, my developer sent this warning, and I am not sure what they need me to choose.
whyItWorks: The result is not generic reassurance; it is the client-owned choice hiding inside implementation language.

eyebrow: Agreement needs a visible test
headline: Turn meeting agreement into evidence everyone can recognize.
subheadline: Paste the line everyone agreed to. Together we'll define what someone would see when it is true, who can show it, and what would disprove it.
chatStarter: Rustam, the meeting ended in agreement, but I doubt we would recognize success the same way.
whyItWorks: It converts polite consensus into a shared test, reducing the chance that agreement hides different expectations.

eyebrow: Finish means serving a narrow use
headline: Cut the side build to a finishable promise.
subheadline: Show me what the build already does and the person it is meant to help. We'll choose a narrow promise, remove the ornamental scope, and define the evidence for done.
chatStarter: Rustam, my side build keeps growing because every possible user suggests another feature.
whyItWorks: A narrow user promise gives the build an honest stopping point without asking the creator to abandon it.

eyebrow: Let failure reduce uncertainty
headline: Use the failed attempt to remove an assumption.
subheadline: Describe what you expected, what happened, and which assumption the attempt actually touched. I'll mark what the evidence can remove and what still needs testing.
chatStarter: Rustam, the attempt failed, but I do not want to throw away the information it produced.
whyItWorks: The failed attempt gains value through a smaller assumption set, not through forced optimism.

eyebrow: Automate repetition, preserve discernment
headline: Separate repeatable steps from judgment calls worth keeping.
subheadline: Paste the workflow as people perform it today. I'll mark stable rules, context-heavy choices, and the handoff where automation should ask for a human.
chatStarter: Rustam, I want to automate this flow without flattening the parts that require experience.
whyItWorks: The separation produces a safer automation boundary instead of treating every manual step as waste.

eyebrow: Make the cost legible across roles
headline: Turn the tradeoff into consequences each person can see.
subheadline: Name the options and the people affected. Together we'll write what each option improves, worsens, delays, and makes harder to reverse.
chatStarter: Rustam, both options sound reasonable because different people are carrying different costs.
proofLine: Start with a free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: Role-specific consequences make a difficult choice discussable without manufacturing certainty.

eyebrow: A useful test separates explanations
headline: Turn the recurring symptom into a discriminating test.
subheadline: Send examples of when the symptom appears and when it does not. I'll frame competing explanations and identify an observation that would make them diverge.
chatStarter: Rustam, this symptom keeps returning, and every explanation seems to fit after the fact.
whyItWorks: The test promises evidence that separates explanations, not an instant diagnosis from a single message.
